exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Use the word given in capitals to form a word
exercises.title: A: Use the word given in capitals to form a word
exercises.instructions: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap.

items.sentence: My sister has always dreamed of becoming a professional (1) ...... (ENTERTAIN), so she signed up for a performing arts course as soon as she left school.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is person who performs, so the correct answer is "entertainer".

wrongOptions.1: The bare infinitive (1) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (1) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: After a lengthy (2) ...... (CONVERSE) with her drama tutor about the (3) ...... (BORE) of doing the same routines night after night, she decided to write her own material and start booking solo (4) ...... (PERFORM) at comedy clubs around the city.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from CONVERSE, so the correct answer is "conversation, boredom, performances".

wrongOptions.2: The bare infinitive (2) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (2) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: She's doing brilliantly! She is (5) ...... (CURRENT) rehearsing a brand new show that she hopes to take on a national tour.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adverb from CURRENT, so the correct answer is "currently".

wrongOptions.5: The bare infinitive (5) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (5) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: There was genuine (6) ...... (EXCITE) in the theatre when the director announced the names of the lead cast members.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from EXCITE, so the correct answer is "excitement".

wrongOptions.6: The bare infinitive (6) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (6) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: A great stand-up comedian needs real (7) ...... (VARY) in their set, mixing stories the crowd finds (8) ...... (AMUSE) with sharp impressions of (9) ...... (FAME) politicians, and always keeping the audience (10) ...... (ACT) engaged until the very last punchline.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from VARY, so the correct answer is "variety, amusing, famous, actively".

wrongOptions.7: The bare infinitive (7) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: A finished past-time expression usually requires the past simple, not a perfect tense.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (7) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.title: B: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.

items.sentence:
We didn't throw an after-show party for the cast; we took them out for dinner instead. **instead**
We took the cast out for dinner ...... an after-show party.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is instead of, so the correct answer is "instead of having".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "instead of having", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
It doesn't surprise me that Sandra missed every single rehearsal. **just**
It ...... miss every single rehearsal.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is typical behaviour, so the correct answer is "is just like Sandra to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
I'm sure the fans will go wild when the headline act walks on stage. **bound**
The fans ...... wild when the headline act walks on stage.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is certain to happen, so the correct answer is "are bound to go".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "are bound to go", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
You shouldn't record videos during a live concert. **supposed**
You ...... videos during a live concert.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not allowed, so the correct answer is "are not supposed to take".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "are not supposed to take", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
At least she said she was sorry that she'd ruined the film's ending! **apologised**
At least she ...... the film's ending!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is said sorry, so the correct answer is "apologised for ruining".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "apologised for ruining", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
The producers don't mind you attending the premiere with the cast. **happy**
The producers ...... attend the premiere with the cast.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is don't mind, so the correct answer is "are happy for you to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "are happy for you to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Don't tease Marcus about his stage fright as he doesn't like it. **fun**
Don't ...... Marcus about his stage fright as he doesn't like it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is tease, so the correct answer is "make fun of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "make fun of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "make fun of" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence:
That live concert really affected me. **impression**
That live concert really ...... me.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is affected deeply, so the correct answer is "made an impression on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "made an impression on", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Write a phrasal verb in the correct form to replace the words in bold
exercises.title: C: Write a phrasal verb in the correct form to replace the words in bold
exercises.instructions: Write a phrasal verb in the correct form to replace the words in bold.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write a phrasal verb in the correct form to replace the words in bold.

items.sentence: My uncle fell asleep during the ballet and started snoring loudly in the front row!
items.explanation: Fell asleep → **dropped off** during the ballet.

items.sentence: I felt so disappointed when the lead singer cancelled the show just two hours before it was due to start.
items.explanation: Felt disappointed → felt **let down** when the show was cancelled.

items.sentence: Despite touring together for years, the two band members apparently don't have a good relationship with each other offstage.
items.explanation: Have a good relationship → don't **get along** offstage.

items.sentence: The drama club is going to perform a musical at the end of the school year.
items.explanation: Perform a show → **put on** a musical.

items.sentence: Danny resembles his grandfather; they both share a passion for classic jazz music.
The presenter told the audience that the show had been cancelled and everyone believed it!
items.explanation: Believed a trick → everyone **fell for** the prank.

items.sentence: Buster Keaton will always be remembered as one of the greatest comic talents in the history of silent film.
items.explanation: Be remembered → will **go down** in history.

items.sentence: You can always trust the orchestra to deliver a stunning performance at the annual gala.
items.explanation: Trust → you can **count on** the orchestra.
